project.title: Counterfactual Audio
project.paper_title: Learning Audio Concepts from Counterfactual Natural Language
project.summary: Counterfactual caption release for AudioCaps, Clotho, and MACS, prepared for the ICASSP 2024 paper on audio-language learning with alternative linguistic scenarios.
project.novelty_statement: This release adds counterfactual language for the same audio clip, not just factual caption matching.
project.retrieval_gain: 43%+ top-1 improvement on Clotho text-to-audio retrieval

prompts: Audio signal is a kind of wave that is directional from the source to microphone. Then, microphone records the sound. Each sound may have multiple sound sources. Humans detect these sounds and describe it based on its sources. What are the sound source or sources in the given caption?
Alter these sources with something very different and generate a caption of the same length and in one line and without itemization.

datasets.name: AudioCaps
datasets.notes: One factual caption and one counterfactual caption per clip.

examples.factual: Idling car, train blows horn and passes
examples.counterfactual: Dogs barking, thunder rumbles in the distance

examples.factual: A racing vehicle engine is heard passing by
examples.counterfactual: A group of children laughing and playing in a park.

examples.factual: A waterfall is pouring into a stream
examples.counterfactual: A guitar is being strummed in the studio.

examples.factual: Water flows as a child shouts in the background
examples.counterfactual: Wind howls as a guitar strums in the distance.

datasets.name: Clotho
datasets.notes: Five factual captions and five aligned counterfactual captions per clip.

examples.factual: A muddled noise of broken channel of the TV
examples.counterfactual: A tranquil symphony of ocean waves and seagulls at the beach.

examples.factual: A stick strikes a percussive instrument creating reverb
examples.counterfactual: A gentle breeze whispers through the leaves creating a soothing rustle.

examples.factual: The squeaking of an old door opening, rustling ensues, the door squeaks again, and a woman speaks.
examples.counterfactual: The ringing of church bells, chirping of birds, rustling of leaves, and a man whistles.

examples.factual: Dishes click and clack in the background as a crowd chatters indistinctly.
examples.counterfactual: Sirens wail and helicopters thud in the foreground as a journalist interviews a witness.

datasets.name: Clotho
datasets.notes: In the current release, captions_counterfactual matches captions for every paired entry.

datasets.name: MACS
datasets.notes: Multi-annotator captions paired with aligned counterfactual rewrites.

examples.factual: a person whistling and singing
examples.counterfactual: A violin playing and a clock ticking.

examples.factual: adults talking and kids screaming in the background at the beginning a rattling sound occurs
examples.counterfactual: musical instruments playing and people singing in the background at the beginning a phone ringing sound occurs

examples.factual: adults talking with cars accelerating in the background
examples.counterfactual: Musicians jamming with dogs barking in the background.

examples.factual: adults talking and announcement jingle
examples.counterfactual: rain pouring and thunder rumbling

showcases.label: Source / event swaps
showcases.title: When the clip stays fixed but the core source changes
showcases.description: These pairs mostly alter a salient source or event while leaving the caption format natural and compact.
showcases.note: This view is useful for researchers who want text-first hard negatives that stay close to the original caption style.

examples.factual: The dog is whimpering sadly
examples.counterfactual: The violin is playing melodiously.

examples.factual: Water is bubbling
examples.counterfactual: Music is playing.

examples.factual: A stick strikes a percussive instrument creating reverb
examples.counterfactual: A gentle breeze whispers through the leaves creating a soothing rustle.

examples.factual: Loud television static dips in and out of focus
examples.counterfactual: Gentle raindrops pitter-patter against the windowpane.

examples.focus: whistling and singing to violin and clock
examples.factual: a person whistling and singing
examples.counterfactual: A violin playing and a clock ticking.

examples.focus: public-space chatter to dog bark and loud music
examples.factual: a child screaming people talking in the background
examples.counterfactual: a dog barking music playing loudly from a nearby house

showcases.label: Scene-level rewrites
showcases.title: When the counterfactual changes the whole scene
showcases.description: These examples shift multiple sound sources at once, turning the same audio path into a linguistically different scene.
showcases.note: This view highlights why counterfactual language is stronger than a generic random mismatch: the alternatives are still fluent scene descriptions.

examples.focus: vehicle pass-by to children in a park
examples.factual: A racing vehicle engine is heard passing by
examples.counterfactual: A group of children laughing and playing in a park.

examples.focus: water and child to wind and distant guitar
examples.factual: Water flows as a child shouts in the background
examples.counterfactual: Wind howls as a guitar strums in the distance.

examples.focus: door, leaves, and speech to bells, birds, and whistle
examples.factual: The squeaking of an old door opening, rustling ensues, the door squeaks again, and a woman speaks.
examples.counterfactual: The ringing of church bells, chirping of birds, rustling of leaves, and a man whistles.

examples.factual: Dishes click and clack in the background as a crowd chatters indistinctly.
examples.counterfactual: Sirens wail and helicopters thud in the foreground as a journalist interviews a witness.

examples.factual: adults talking and announcement jingle
examples.counterfactual: rain pouring and thunder rumbling

examples.focus: public square to traffic and construction
examples.factual: multiple sounds of people talking and children playing in the background
examples.counterfactual: A cacophony of car honking and construction work in progress near the vicinity.

showcases.label: Dataset-specific examples
showcases.title: Representative pairs from AudioCaps, Clotho, and MACS
showcases.description: This view shows how the release differs by source dataset, from single-caption AudioCaps clips to multi-caption Clotho and multi-annotator MACS.
showcases.note: The current Clotho validation release is excluded here because its counterfactual field mirrors the factual captions.

examples.factual: Idling car, train blows horn and passes
examples.counterfactual: Dogs barking, thunder rumbles in the distance

examples.focus: single-caption pair with natural ambience shift
examples.factual: Water is running and birds are chirping in the background
examples.counterfactual: Music is playing and children are laughing in the background.

examples.focus: one of five aligned caption pairs
examples.factual: A muddled noise of broken channel of the TV
examples.counterfactual: A tranquil symphony of ocean waves and seagulls at the beach.

examples.focus: multi-caption contrast on the same clip
examples.factual: Plates and silverware clatter while several people speak.
examples.counterfactual: Church bells chime as birds sing in the background.

examples.factual: people are talking whistling and singing
examples.counterfactual: cars are honking, dogs are barking and birds are chirping.

examples.factual: adults talking with cars accelerating in the background
examples.counterfactual: Musicians jamming with dogs barking in the background.
